macro_segments.title: Ulla's Teaching on Liens: Slaves as Collateral for Debts

micro_segments.title: Opening context and Ulla's statement about collecting debts from slaves
Rav Nachman's challenge and Ulla's clarification about explicit liens
Ulla's true position revealed after Rav Nachman leaves
Practical cases from Neharde'a and Pumbedita supporting liens on slaves
Rav Nachman's strong objection and threat to seize judges' property
Rava challenges Rav Nachman with multiple authorities supporting liens
Rav Nachman's response: he only knows the Braita, not other authorities

macro_segments.title: The Braita on Pruzbul and Liens: Evidence Against Liens on Slaves

micro_segments.title: Braita teaching that Pruzbul works on land but not on slaves
Explanation of Pruzbul principle and why liens matter for its application
Second principle: chattel can be acquired together with land, not with slaves
Analysis: slaves treated differently from both land and chattel
Tosafot's resolution: Slaves are like land only for Torah law, not rabbinic

macro_segments.title: Talmudic Analysis of Two Conflicting Braitot on Acquiring Slaves and Chattel

micro_segments.title: First Braita: taking possession of slaves or land separately, no connection between them
Principle of Kinyan Agav: chattel acquired with land but not vice versa
Slaves with chattel: slaves do not acquire chattel or vice versa
Second Braita introducing alternative: slaves can acquire chattel
Identifying the debate: are slaves like land or like chattel for Kinyan Agav?

macro_segments.title: Rav Ika's First Explanation: Slaves Like Land, Exception for Kinyan Agav

micro_segments.title: Rav Ika: everyone agrees slaves are like land, but Kinyan Agav requires actual property
Verses from Kings about walled cities: basis for Kinyan Agav restriction
Principle: Kinyan Agav needs stable property that doesn't move
Why slaves as moving property cannot serve as vehicle for Kinyan Agav

macro_segments.title: Alternative Explanation: Slaves Like Chattel, Kinyan Chatzer Not Kinyan Agav

micro_segments.title: Rav Ika's alternative: slaves are like chattel, not like land
Second Braita explained through Kinyan Chatzer when property is on the slave
Talmud questions: moving yard—how can Kinyan Chatzer apply to slaves?
Rava's answer: only when slave is bound or asleep does Kinyan Chatzer work
Conclusion: very limited Kinyan Chatzer explanation, not standard Kinyan Agav

macro_segments.title: Reconciling Conflicting Braitot on Acquiring Slaves with Land

micro_segments.title: Third Braita: taking possession of land acquires slaves if standing in the land
Gemara's analysis: if slaves are chattel, standing in land could explain difference
Problem: even if chattel, Kinyan Agav should work without physical proximity
Solution: distinction between moving and non-moving property
If slaves treated as land, they are distinct because they are moving land
Talmudic principle: all land is one unit even across distances, but slaves are separate

macro_segments.title: Summary: Slaves in Liens and Kinyan Context

micro_segments.title: Overall framework: liens question is central to Ulla vs. Rav Nachman debate
Kinyan issues may have exceptions even if slaves like land in principle

macro_segments.title: Transition to New Topic: Nechsim She'ein Bahem Me'ila (Property Exempt from Me'ila)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah introduces principle: payment required for damage to property without me'ila
Gemara asks: what property is sanctified but has no me'ila obligation?
Example of Kodshim Kalim: Shelamim where owner eats meat, no me'ila

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosi HaGlili's Position: Kodshim Kalim as Owner's Property

micro_segments.title: Gemara attributes Mishnah to Rabbi Yosi HaGlili regarding Kodshim Kalim
Rabbi Yosi: Kodshim Kalim considered owner's property, not belonging to Hekdesh
Implication: if ox gores owner's Shelamim, damage payment is owed
Braita on false oath involving property belonging to God and neighbor
Rabbi Yosi extends me'ila concepts to Kodshim Kalim as owner's property

macro_segments.title: Apparent Contradiction: Kiddushin Mishna on Ketubah with Priestly Portion

micro_segments.title: Kiddushin Mishna: priest marrying woman with priestly portion doesn't work
Why doesn't it work? Because priestly portion is not his property
Phrase: eating from God's table, not his own property
Question: doesn't this contradict Rabbi Yosi's view on Kodshim Kalim?

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Rabbi Yosi's Position Limited to Living Animals

micro_segments.title: Gemara: Rabbi Yosi says it's owner's property while alive, not after shechita
After slaughter, it becomes God's portion for priestly eating
Rashi vs. Tosafot: debate on whether owner eating meat is from God's table

macro_segments.title: Firstborn Animal Case: Testing Rabbi Yosi's Position

micro_segments.title: Braita on Bechor: priest can sell it, marry woman with it, seemingly supporting Rabbi Yosi
Rav Nachman limits this: only when Beis HaMikdash doesn't exist
When Beis HaMikdash exists and Bechor could be brought, priest cannot sell or marry
Rava challenges: why can't Rabbi Yosi's principle apply to living Bechor?

macro_segments.title: Final Resolution: Matnas Kehuna vs. Owner's Kodshim

micro_segments.title: Gemara distinguishes: Matnas Kehuna (gifts to priest) are always from God's table
Rabbi Yosi only says owner's Shelamim is owner's property, not priestly portions
Bechor given to priest is Matnas Kehuna, priest is eating from God's table
Therefore Bechor cannot be used for Kiddushin since not priest's actual property

topical_tags.term: Gobvin min ha-avadim (collecting debts from slaves)
Ulla in name of Rabbi Elazar
Pruzbul (rabbinic mechanism to collect after Shemita)
Kinyan Agav (acquisition of chattel with land)
Gneiva v'gezeila (theft and robbery exempt from land/slaves)
Rav Ika's second explanation (slaves like chattel)
Kinyan Chatzer (acquisition of property through a yard/place)
Shmuel's principle (one Karka works for distant Karka)
Sadna de'ara (crust of land as one unit)
Me'ila (sacrilege, inappropriate use of sanctified property)
Nechsim she'ein bahem me'ila (property without me'ila obligation)
Matnas Kehuna (gifts to priest from sacrifices)
Rabbi Shimon's position on Bechor outside Eretz Yisrael
Chazeh v'shok (breast and thigh portions for priests)
